source: ESV Study Bible (Crossway) — Used under license
book_intro: The letter to the Hebrews was written to encourage Christians in a time of trial. It does so by focusing on the absolute supremacy and sufficiency of Jesus Christ. While God spoke in the past "many times and in many ways," he has now spoken to us "by his Son," Jesus Christ, who is the "exact imprint" of God's nature and who "upholds the universe by the word of his power" (1:1-3). Jesus accomplished complete salvation for all who trust in him (1:1-10:18). We dare not "neglect such a great salvation" (2:3; 5:12-6:20; 10:19-39). Rather, in our faith and in our everyday living, we should imitate the example of Christ and of those on the honor roll of faith (chs. 11-13). This letter, whose author is unknown, was probably written between a.d. 60 and 70.
chapter_intro: The 'rest' promised in Ps 95 — and earlier in the land promise to Israel — remains open, the author argues, because Joshua's conquest did not exhaust it. A Sabbath rest still awaits the people of God, and the way to enter it is the same as it always was: faith in God's word. The chapter closes with a transitional paragraph that reintroduces the theme of the great high priest, this time emphasizing his empathy: he was tempted in every way, yet without sin, and therefore we can approach with confidence.

passages.title: The Sabbath Rest Remains
passages.text: The argument is intricate: God's rest was established at creation (Gen 2:2), yet Ps 95 — written centuries after Joshua's conquest — still says 'today.' This implies the rest was not yet entered, and it remains available 'today' to those who receive the promise with faith. The Christian Sabbath-rest is entered in part through faith now and fully in eschatological completion.

passages.title: The Living and Active Word
passages.text: A parenthetical but vital warning: God's word lays bare the state of every soul. The image of a two-edged sword cutting between soul and spirit, joints and marrow, is not anatomical analysis but a description of Scripture's ability to expose the true condition of the heart — including whether one's faith is genuine or merely nominal.

passages.title: Draw Near with Confidence
passages.text: The exhortation to 'hold fast our confession' and 'draw near with confidence' flows from the character of our high priest: he has passed through the heavens (entered God's presence as our forerunner) and was tempted as we are, yet without sin. His sinlessness means he sympathizes without being contaminated; his priestly intercession means mercy and grace are available precisely when we need them.

notes.text: the message they heard did not benefit them — The word was preached to the wilderness generation, but it was not 'mixed with faith' by those who heard it. Hearing the gospel is not the same as believing it.
a Sabbath rest — The Greek sabbatismos appears only here in the NT. It carries overtones of the ultimate Sabbath — the eschatological rest inaugurated by Christ's work.
living and active — The word of God is not a static artifact but a living agent. 'Active' (energēs) means at work, effective, operational.
tempted as we are, yet without sin — The 'yet' is crucial. Jesus' sinlessness does not diminish his sympathy; it deepens it. He faced every pull toward sin without ever yielding.
throne of grace — Not a throne of judgment (which it might be for sinners), but grace — because the one who sits on it is also our high priest and advocate.
